title: The Student Who Walked Through Walls
content:
The carriage stopped at the foot of Cloud Summit Mountain, and Bai Su's father handed her the bamboo basket with two days' worth of dried mantou. "Study hard," he said. "Make us proud."

She nodded, watching his cart rattle back down the winding dirt road until dust swallowed it whole. When she turned to face the academy gates, her stomach did something unpleasant.

Wutong Academy rose from the mountain mist like a sleeping beast. Its walls were weathered grey stone, crawling with moss so old it had turned black. The main gate stood open—two massive wooden doors carved with scenes of scholars taking examinations, their brush strokes frozen mid-character. Above the entrance, a plaque declared in gold-leaf calligraphy: BY KNOWLEDGE, EVEN THE DEAD MAY PASS.

Bai Su shifted her bundle on her shoulder and walked through.

The courtyard sprawled before her, cobblestones slick with morning dew. A gingko tree dominated the center, its leaves just beginning to yellow at the edges. To her left, a lecture hall with paper windows. To her right, dormitories built in the southern style, their curved eaves reaching toward the grey sky.

Students milled about in small clusters. Most wore the same plain grey robes she did—new students, like her. A few older ones in blue carried scrolls and debated something about the Analects.

Bai Su took a breath. She could do this. She had passed the entrance examination. She belonged here.

A boy in grey robes walked straight toward the gingko tree.

Not around it. Through it.

The trunk swallowed him whole for one impossible heartbeat, then spat him out the other side. He emerged with his hair slightly tousled, adjusting his collar as if nothing had happened.

Bai Su's bundle hit the cobblestones.

She stared.

A girl beside her—living, judging by the warmth radiating from her skin—glanced over. "Are you alright?"

"That student," Bai Su managed. "He just walked through—"

She stopped. The girl was looking at her like she'd grown a second head.

"Walked through what?"

Bai Su turned back to the gingko tree.

The boy was staring directly at her.

His eyes widened. His mouth formed a perfect O.

Then he started walking toward her.

Through three other students. One of them shivered and pulled his collar tighter, muttering about the mountain cold.

"Don't," Bai Su whispered, but her voice came out strangled.

Too late.

The boy reached her, stopping barely a foot away. Up close, she could see the blue tint to his lips. The way the morning light passed through him, casting no shadow on the stones beneath his feet. The lingering smell of river water and something rotting.

"You can see me?" he asked.

His voice sounded like stones dropping into a deep well.

The girl beside Bai Su looked at her funny. "See who?"

"No one," Bai Su said quickly. "I'm fine. Just tired from the journey."

The girl shrugged and walked away, joining a group of new students heading toward the dormitories.

Bai Su stood frozen, staring at the ghost.

"You can actually see me," he repeated, wonder coloring his hollow voice. "That's— that's never happened before. I've been here for—" He paused, counting on his fingers. "—twelve years? No, thirteen. Time gets strange." He laughed, a wet, gurgling sound. "What's your name?"

"Bai Su."

"Bai Su." He tested the name on his tongue, then smiled. It was not a reassuring smile. His teeth were grey. "Welcome to Wutong Academy. I'm Liu Wei. I drowned in the river behind the east library during my third year. Failed the provincial examination three times before that. The water gets into your lungs and you realize all those Confucian maxims don't help you swim."

Bai Su's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against her thighs.

"I'm not supposed to be able to see you," she said. "Only monks and priests can see ghosts. I'm just a student."

Liu Wei tilted his head. "And yet."

She forced herself to breathe. In through the nose. Out through the mouth. Her mother's voice echoed from childhood: *When you panic, anchor yourself to something solid.*

She anchored herself to the cobblestones beneath her feet. The weight of her bundle. The distant sound of a bell ringing from the lecture hall.

"I need to find my dormitory," she said.

"Ding Hall." Liu Wei pointed toward the western buildings. "Third floor, fourth door on the left. Your roommate's name is Chen Xiaolian. She bites her nails when she studies. She's also the top new student this year."

Bai Su stared at him. "How do you know that?"

"I've been here thirteen years." Liu Wei shrugged, the motion sending ripples through his semi-transparent form. "I know everything about this academy. The living tend to forget ghosts are listening."

She should run. She should find a teacher and tell them she was hallucinating. She should—

"You should probably go," Liu Wei said, his voice dropping. "The Dean's morning assembly starts in fifteen minutes. First-day lateness gets you three strikes."

"How do you know the schedule?"

"I told you." His smile turned strange. "I know everything."

Bai Su grabbed her bundle and walked toward the western dormitories. She didn't run. Running would draw attention. But her legs moved faster than they needed to.

Behind her, she heard Liu Wei's voice float through the air: "See you around, Bai Su. We have so much to discuss."

She didn't turn back.

---

Ding Hall was a three-story wooden building that groaned in the mountain wind. Bai Su climbed the creaking stairs to the third floor, found the fourth door on the left, and pushed it open.

The room was small. Two beds. Two desks. A window that looked out onto the gingko tree. A girl was already there, hunched over a stack of books, her fingers in her mouth.

She looked up. Young, maybe fifteen, with sharp eyes and hair pulled into a severe bun.

"Chen Xiaolian?" Bai Su asked.

The girl nodded, pulling her fingers out of her mouth. "You're Bai Su. I saw your name on the roster." She gestured at the empty bed. "That one's yours. Don't put anything on the desk—I need the space for my study materials."

Bai Su set down her bundle. Her hands were still shaking.

Chen Xiaolian noticed. "Nervous?"

"Something like that."

"You shouldn't be. The entrance exam is the hardest part. If you passed that, the classes are manageable." She turned back to her books. "I've already read the first three chapters of the Confucian classics. If you need help catching up, I charge two copper coins per tutoring session."

Bai Su sat on the edge of her bed. The straw mattress rustled beneath her.

"Have you noticed anything strange about the academy?" she asked.

Chen Xiaolian didn't look up. "Strange how?"

"The students. Some of them seem..." Bai Su searched for the right word. "Cold."

"Cold?"

"Like they don't cast shadows."

Now Chen Xiaolian looked up. Her eyes narrowed. "What are you talking about?"

Bai Su hesitated. She barely knew this girl. But she had to tell someone. The weight of what she'd seen pressed against her chest like a stone.

"I saw a student walk through a tree," she said. "Through it. Not around. He disappeared into the trunk and came out the other side."

Chen Xiaolian stared at her for a long moment.

Then she laughed.

"That's ridiculous," she said, shaking her head. "You're probably just tired. The journey up the mountain is exhausting. I nearly fell asleep standing up when I arrived."

"It's not exhaustion. I know what I saw."

Chen Xiaolian's laughter faded. She studied Bai Su's face, her expression shifting from amusement to something more serious.

"There are rumors," she said slowly. "About this academy."

Bai Su leaned forward. "What rumors?"

"That some of the students never leave. That they stay here, year after year, even after graduation." Chen Xiaolian lowered her voice. "They say the library has books no living person has touched in decades. That the west wing is always cold, even in summer. That sometimes, at night, you can hear students reciting the classics in rooms that should be empty."

Bai Su's blood went cold.

"That's just stories," she said, but her voice wavered.

"Probably." Chen Xiaolian turned back to her books. "Every old school has its ghost stories. It doesn't mean they're true."

The bell rang. Three clear chimes.

"Assembly," Chen Xiaolian said, standing. "Come on. You don't want to be late."

Bai Su followed her out of the room, down the creaking stairs, across the courtyard toward the main lecture hall. The gingko tree cast its shadow over the cobblestones. She counted the students as they walked.

Thirty-seven in grey robes, new students.

Twelve in blue, older students.

And six others.

They walked together, their grey robes identical to hers. But they cast no shadows. Their feet made no sound on the stones. Their breath didn't fog in the cool mountain air.

None of them looked at her.

But she felt them watching.

---

The assembly was held in the Great Hall, a cavernous space lined with bamboo scrolls and portraits of scholars long dead. Candles flickered on every surface, casting dancing shadows across the walls.

Dean Weng stood at the front. Old, with a face like wrinkled parchment and eyes that seemed to look through you rather than at you.

"Welcome," he said, his voice carrying through the hall without effort, "to Wutong Academy. You have been chosen because you have potential. Because you have the discipline to succeed. Because you will pass the imperial examinations and bring honor to your families."

He paused, scanning the room. His gaze passed over Bai Su, and she felt a chill run down her spine.

"However," he continued, "success at Wutong comes with rules. First: you will not enter the west wing after dark. Second: you will not disturb the scholars in the library. Third"—his eyes grew sharp—"you will not speak of what you see."

Murmurs rippled through the students.

Bai Su's heart hammered.

"Some of you will notice things that seem unnatural," Dean Weng said. "You will hear whispers from empty rooms. You will see figures in the corner of your vision. You will feel cold hands brush your shoulders when no one is there."

He smiled. It did not reach his eyes.

"This is normal. Do not acknowledge it. Do not speak of it. Focus on your studies, and the academy will reward you. Disturb what should not be disturbed, and you will learn why the west wing is forbidden."

The hall fell silent.

Bai Su looked around at the other students. Some looked frightened. Some looked confused. A few—the ones who cast no shadows—looked amused.

Chen Xiaolian stood beside her, biting her nails.

"This is insane," she whispered. "What kind of school is this?"

Bai Su didn't answer.

Because behind Dean Weng, in the shadows of the stage, stood Liu Wei.

The drowned scholar.

He raised his hand and waved.

Then he pressed a finger to his grey lips.

*Don't tell,* the gesture said. *Or else.*

The assembly ended.

The students filed out, chattering nervously about the Dean's warning. Chen Xiaolian grabbed Bai Su's arm and pulled her toward the dining hall.

"Come on. I heard they serve pork buns today."

Bai Su let herself be dragged

title: The First Victim
content:
Bai Su's first class at Wutong Academy was calligraphy.

The classroom smelled of ink and age, sunlight slanting through paper windows to paint golden rectangles across the floor. Thirty desks sat in neat rows. Twenty-nine students waited.

One desk stood empty.

The master hadn't arrived yet. Bai Su kept her eyes forward, her hands folded, her breathing measured. She'd learned long ago that stillness was armor. That watching without being watched was the only way to survive.

"New girl."

The voice came from her left. A boy with sharp features and sharper eyes leaned toward her. His robes were blue silk, expensive, his hair bound with a jade guan that probably cost more than her family's house.

"Bai Su," she said.

"Zhou Yan." He smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. "You're the one from the south. The scholarship student."

Not a question. He'd already done his research.

"Yes."

"Interesting." He tapped his brush against his desk. "Most scholarship students don't last the first month. They find the atmosphere... unsettling."

Bai Su said nothing.

Zhou Yan's smile widened. "Or maybe you'll be different. You seem to have a talent for seeing things others miss."

Her blood chilled. She kept her face blank.

"I don't know what you mean."

"Of course you don't." He turned back to his paper, dismissing her. "Welcome to Wutong Academy, Bai Su. Try not to die."

The master arrived before she could respond. Old man Liu, they called him—a stooped figure with ink-stained fingers and eyes that had seen too much. He set down his materials without greeting, without preamble.

"Copy the Thousand Character Classic," he said. "Twice. Begin."

Brushes scratched across paper. The room settled into the rhythm of work, the familiar scrape and flow that Bai Su had practiced since she was five years old. She dipped her brush, centered her mind, and wrote.

*Heaven and earth, dark and yellow.*

The characters flowed from her brush like water. She'd always been good at this. The only thing she'd ever been good at.

*The universe, vast and wild.*

Movement caught her eye. The empty desk.

A girl sat there now.

Bai Su's brush froze. The girl was young, maybe fourteen, with a pale face and dark hair that fell unbound past her shoulders. She wore the same academy robes as everyone else, but they hung wet, dripping water onto the floor that no one else seemed to notice.

She was writing.

The girl's brush moved across the paper, character after character, each one perfect. She didn't look up. Didn't acknowledge Bai Su's stare.

"Is there a problem, Miss Bai?"

Old Man Liu's voice cut through her trance. Bai Su turned to find him standing beside her desk, his ancient eyes fixed on her face.

"No, Master Liu."

"Then continue."

She looked back at the empty desk.

The girl was gone.

---

The drowned scholar had told her the truth.

Bai Su found him in the library that evening, hunched over a copy of the *Analects* that he'd been reading for three hundred years. He looked up when she entered, his translucent face splitting into a smile that showed teeth that were slightly too long.

"New student," he said. "You came back."

"I need information."

"Everyone needs information." He closed his book with a sound like wet paper. "The question is what you're willing to pay."

Bai Su set a stick of incense on the table. Sandalwood, good quality, stolen from the shrine in the east courtyard.

The drowned scholar's eyes lit up. He leaned forward, breathing deep as she lit the incense, watching the smoke curl toward the ceiling. His form flickered, became more solid.

"What do you want to know?"

"Who was the girl in calligraphy class?"

The scholar's expression shifted. Something like fear, or maybe hunger, passed across his features.

"Ah. You saw her."

"Yes."

"Most don't. Not even the ones who can see us." He tapped his fingers on the table, a nervous gesture that sent ripples through his ghostly form. "She died seven years ago. Hanged herself in the west wing after failing her examinations. Her family had mortgaged everything for her education. When she failed, they had nothing."

"Her name?"

"Lin Xue."

Bai Su memorized it. "Why was she in my class?"

The drowned scholar leaned back, his eyes distant. "She never leaves. She sits in that desk every day, every lesson, writing characters that no one reads. She's been doing it for seven years. She'll probably do it for seven hundred more."

"Because she failed?"

"Because she can't accept it." His voice dropped. "There are many like her here. Ghosts who died before they could take the exams, who haunt the academy hoping for another chance. But Lin Xue... she's different."

"How?"

"She's been getting stronger."

The words hung in the air. Bai Su's hand tightened on her sleeve.

"Stronger how?"

"Stronger in the way that matters." The drowned scholar's eyes met hers, and for a moment she saw something ancient and hungry looking out from them. "Ghosts feed on life qi. Most of us barely survive on the ambient energy that leaks from living students. But Lin Xue has been feeding. Properly. Deliberately."

A cold knot formed in Bai Su's stomach. "You think she's the one killing students?"

"I think," the drowned scholar said, "that you should be very careful who you trust."

The library bell rang. Closing time. Bai Su stood, but the scholar caught her wrist with fingers that felt like cold water.

"One more thing."

"What?"

"The boy who died in the examination hall. The one they found with his throat cut." His grip tightened. "He wasn't the first. And he won't be the last."

"Who else has died?"

"Check the academy records. The ones they don't show new students." He released her, fading back into his chair. "Look for the pattern. Look for the names that disappear."

Bai Su left the library with her heart pounding and her mind racing.

She didn't see the figure watching her from the shadows of the west wing.

---

The dormitory was quiet when she returned.

Five girls shared her room, all of them asleep or pretending to be. Bai Su lay on her cot, staring at the ceiling, listening to the wind howl outside. The academy felt different at night. Older. Hungrier.

She thought about Lin Xue, the drowned girl who sat in class every day writing characters no one would ever read. She thought about the murdered student, his throat cut in the examination hall. She thought about the pattern the scholar had mentioned, the names that disappeared.

Sleep came slowly, and when it came, it brought dreams.

She was standing in the examination hall. Hundreds of desks stretched into darkness, each one occupied by a figure in white. They were all writing, brushes moving in perfect synchronization, the scratch of ink against paper filling the air like insect wings.

At the front of the hall stood a figure in red.

Lin Xue.

But different. Her hair was bound, her robes immaculate, her face painted with the makeup of a bride. She held a scroll in her hands, unrolled it, and began to read.

*Bai Su, daughter of Bai Cheng, born in the year of the Rabbit. Failed the village examination at age twelve. Failed again at fifteen. Failed again at eighteen. Admitted to Wutong Academy on a scholarship of pity.*

The words echoed through the hall. The writing figures stopped. Turned.

Their faces were blank. No eyes, no mouths, just smooth white skin stretched over bone.

*Bai Su, who sees what should not be seen.*

*Bai Su, who knows what should not be known.*

*Bai Su, who will die before the first snow.*

Lin Xue's voice changed. Became deeper. Older. Hungrier.

"Unless."

Bai Su tried to move, but her feet were rooted to the floor. The faceless figures rose from their desks, drifting toward her, their hands reaching.

"Unless you give me what I want."

"What do you want?" Bai Su's voice came out as a whisper.

Lin Xue smiled.

It was the smile of something that had stopped being human a long time ago.

"Your life."

---

Bai Su woke gasping.

The dormitory was dark. Silent. The other girls hadn't stirred. She lay there, her heart hammering, her skin slick with sweat, trying to convince herself it had just been a dream.

Then she saw the water.

A puddle spread across the floor beside her cot. Fresh. Clear. As if someone had been standing there, dripping, watching her sleep.

And on the wall above her head, written in wet characters that gleamed in the darkness:

**SEVEN DAYS.**

title: The Blood-Soaked Examination Hall
content:
Bai Su's knuckles were still white when the morning bell rang.

She hadn't slept. Every creak of the wooden floorboards had sent her heart hammering against her ribs. The girl in the courtyard had simply vanished after whispering those words—*three days*—leaving nothing but the smell of wet earth and old incense.

Now she sat in the main hall, surrounded by forty-seven students eating their morning rice porridge. Forty-seven living students. She'd counted twice.

The ghosts were easier to spot in daylight.

They sat at separate tables near the windows, their forms translucent in the morning sun. A young man with a gaping wound across his throat kept trying to drink his tea, the liquid spilling through his neck each time. A woman in burial robes read a book whose pages were blank to Bai Su but seemed to contain something meaningful to her dead eyes.

"The drowned scholar," she whispered.

"What?" Lin Wei sat down beside her, bowl in hand. "Talking to yourself already? The pressure's getting to you."

Bai Su forced a smile. "Just tired."

"You look like you haven't slept." Lin Wei's voice carried genuine concern. "First night in the dorms gets everyone. Strange place, strange sounds—"

"I heard a woman crying in the courtyard."

Lin Wei's chopsticks stopped halfway to her mouth. "You heard that too?"

The air between them thickened. Bai Su's pulse quickened.

"You've heard it?"

"Everyone has." Lin Wei lowered her voice, leaning close. "They say it's the ghost of a student who hanged herself after failing the exams. Forty years ago. She wanders the academy at night, weeping for the answers she never learned."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "Does she speak?"

"Some say she whispers the exam questions to those who listen. But no one's ever passed after hearing them." Lin Wei shuddered. "They always go mad first."

*You have three days to leave this place.*

The words echoed in Bai Su's skull. She gripped her chopsticks until her fingertips ached.

"Lin Wei, do you believe in ghosts?"

The question hung between them. Around them, the hall buzzed with morning chatter, the clatter of bowls, the scrape of benches.

Lin Wei's face went still. "My grandmother used to say that the dead don't rest until their business is finished. And in a place like this..." She nodded toward the west wing, where shadows seemed to pool even in daylight. "There's a lot of unfinished business."

"Master Zhao says this academy has a hundred percent pass rate."

"Master Zhao says a lot of things." Lin Wei stood, picking up her bowl. "He doesn't say how many students disappear before they can take the exams."

She walked away before Bai Su could ask what she meant.

---

The morning lecture was held in the Grand Study Hall, a cavernous room lined with scrolls and books that seemed to breathe with their own life. Dust motes danced in the slivers of light piercing through paper windows.

Master Zhao stood at the front, his robes immaculate, his face a mask of scholarly benevolence. He was writing Confucian maxims on the blackboard with practiced strokes.

Bai Su couldn't focus.

She kept scanning the room, counting the living students again. Forty-seven. The ghosts were harder to count—they flickered in and out of her vision, some sitting among the living, others hovering near the ceiling.

One ghost in particular caught her attention.

He sat in the back corner, separate from the others. Unlike the translucent forms of the other dead, he seemed almost solid—a young man in scholar's robes, his face sharp and handsome, his eyes dark as ink stones. He was writing notes with a brush that left no marks on the page.

When he looked up, his gaze met hers directly.

Bai Su's breath caught.

He could see her.

The ghost smiled—a thin, knowing expression—and returned to his writing.

"Miss Bai."

Master Zhao's voice cut through her thoughts. She snapped to attention.

"Perhaps you can explain the principle of rectifying names?"

The room went quiet. Forty-seven living students turned to look at her. Somewhere, a ghost laughed.

Bai Su's mind raced. Rectifying names. She'd read about it in her father's books. The Confucian idea that things should be called by their proper names, that order comes from correct naming—

"If names are not correct, speech will not be effective," she said slowly, reciting from memory. "If speech is not effective, affairs cannot be accomplished. If affairs cannot be accomplished, rites and music will not flourish."

Master Zhao nodded, his expression unreadable. "And what does this mean for a student of Wutong Academy?"

"It means..." Bai Su's eyes drifted to the ghost in the corner, who was watching her with obvious amusement. "It means we must call things what they truly are. A student must be called a student. A teacher must be called a teacher."

"And a ghost?" Master Zhao's voice dropped, barely a whisper. "What do we call a ghost?"

The temperature in the room plummeted.

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"Master Zhao—"

"You see them, don't you?" The headmaster's eyes bore into hers, ancient and knowing. "You've seen them since you arrived. The ones who sit among the living but do not breathe. The ones who read books that do not exist."

The other students exchanged confused glances. They couldn't hear this. They couldn't hear any of it.

"Yes," Bai Su said, her voice steady despite the trembling in her hands. "I see them."

Master Zhao set down his brush. The sound echoed through the silent hall.

"Then you understand the true purpose of this academy." He walked toward her, his footsteps measured. "We do not merely educate the living. We give peace to the dead. Those who died before their time, before their exams, before their names could be written in the annals of scholars. Here, they have one more chance."

"At what?"

"At finishing their business." Master Zhao stopped before her desk. "At passing the imperial examinations. At earning the right to leave this world and enter the next."

Bai Su's mind reeled. "You're saying the ghosts—the dead students—they're here to take the exams?"

"Every year, five of them are chosen." Master Zhao's voice dropped to a whisper that only she could hear. "They sit the imperial examinations alongside the living. And if they pass, their names are recorded in heaven. They move on from this world."

"And if they fail?"

His face darkened. "Some of them have been here for two hundred years."

A chill crept down Bai Su's spine. "The girl in the courtyard. She said I have three days to leave."

"The dead are territorial." Master Zhao straightened, his voice returning to its normal tone. "They see the living as threats, as competitors for a chance at the examinations. Some of them will try to frighten you away."

"Some?"

The ghost in the corner laughed silently, shaking his head.

Master Zhao's eyes flickered toward him, then back to Bai Su. "Finish your studies, Miss Bai. Focus on your lessons. The dead cannot harm you if you do not fear them."

He returned to the front of the room, resuming his lecture as if nothing had happened.

Bai Su sat frozen, her heart pounding. The ghost in the corner was still watching her, still smiling that knowing smile.

When the lecture ended, she grabbed her scrolls and fled.

---

The library was empty when she arrived.

Rows upon rows of books stretched into darkness, their spines cracked and faded with age. Bai Su wandered between them, running her fingers along the leather covers, reading titles that spoke of history and philosophy and the nature of the soul.

She was looking for something specific. She didn't know what until she found it.

A book bound in white silk, tucked between two massive tomes on governance. Its title was written in gold leaf: *Records of the Wutong Academy Dead.*

Bai Su pulled it out, her hands trembling.

The pages were filled with names. Hundreds of them. Some written in fresh ink, others so faded they were barely visible. Each name was followed by a date of death, a cause, and a status.

*Chen Wei, age 19. Drowned in the academy pond, autumn of the Year of the Tiger. Status: Unresolved.*

*Li Hua, age 17. Hanged herself in the west wing, spring of the Year of the Dog. Status: Unresolved.*

*Zhang Feng, age 21. Murdered in the examination hall, winter of the Year of the Rat. Status: Unresolved.*

Page after page of names. Page after page of ghosts.

Bai Su's finger traced the entries, her eyes scanning for—

There.

A name written in blood-red ink, fresh and sharp.

*Wang Xu, age 18. Died in the main hall, three days ago. Status: Resolved.*

Her blood ran cold.

Three days ago. The night before she arrived.

She flipped through more pages, her heart racing. More red ink. More recent entries.

*Liu Mei, age 19. Died in the east dormitory, one week ago. Status: Resolved.*

*Zhao Gang, age 20. Died in the latrine, two weeks ago. Status: Resolved.*

*Sun Li, age 17. Died in the library, three weeks ago. Status: Resolved.*

All resolved. All dead.

And all within the past month.

"Looking for something?"

Bai Su spun around.

The ghost from the lecture hall stood behind her, his dark eyes gleaming in the dim light. He was close enough that she could see the faint blue tinge of his lips, the paleness of his skin beneath his scholar's robes.

"You can see me," he said. It wasn't a question.

"Yes."

"Good." He drifted closer, his feet not quite touching the ground. "Then you're the only one who can help me."

"Help you with what?"

The ghost's smile faded, replaced by something darker. "The other ghosts. The ones who died recently. They didn't pass on naturally."

Bai Su's grip tightened on the book. "What do you mean?"

"I mean someone is killing them." The ghost's voice dropped to a whisper. "Someone is draining their life force, their qi, their very essence. And using it to keep themselves alive."

"Alive?" Bai Su's mind struggled to process the words. "But ghosts are already dead—"

"Some ghosts don't want to pass on." The ghost's eyes flickered toward the library's entrance, as if expecting someone to appear. "Some want to live again. And they'll do anything to achieve it."

"Even kill other ghosts?"

"Even kill the living." The ghost met her gaze, his expression grim. "I've been watching the academy for three hundred years. And in all that time, I've never seen anything like this." He leaned closer, his voice barely a breath. "The girl in the courtyard—the one who warned you—she was one of them. A recent death. A resolved death."

Bai Su's stomach dropped. "She was killed?"

"She was murdered. And her killer is still here." The ghost's eyes glowed faintly in the darkness. "The killer is one of the living students. They've been stealing life force from the dead to extend their own. And they're not done yet."

The library's candle flickered. Shadows danced across the walls.

"Who?" Bai Su asked, her voice barely a whisper. "Who is it?"

The ghost opened his mouth to answer—

A scream tore through the academy.

High and piercing, coming from the main hall.

Bai Su dropped the book and ran.

title: The Weight of a Second Death
content:
The library lamps flickered at dusk.

Bai Su sat cross-legged on the cold stone floor, surrounded by scrolls that smelled of dust and something older—like wet earth after rain. The reading room had emptied an hour ago when the dinner bell rang, but she hadn't moved. Couldn't move. Not with what she'd found.

The drowned scholar floated three shelves away, watching her.

"What?" Bai Su's voice came out raw. "You've been staring for half an hour. Say something."

The boy's lips moved, but only bubbles escaped. He touched his throat—a gesture she'd come to recognize. Ghosts who died by drowning couldn't speak. Couldn't scream. Couldn't warn anyone.

She looked back at the scroll spread across her knees. The academy's death registry, hidden beneath a loose floorboard in the east corridor. She'd found it by accident, chasing a mouse that had no business being that fat. Now she wished she hadn't.

The registry listed every student who died at Wutong Academy in the past fifty years.

Thirty-seven names.

Thirty-seven deaths.

And every single one occurred exactly three days before the imperial examinations.

"There's a pattern," she whispered. Her fingers traced the ink, smudging it. "Three days before exams, someone dies. Always a student who ranked first in the monthly assessments."

The drowned scholar drifted closer. His eyes were dark holes now—no pupils, no whites. Just emptiness that somehow conveyed urgency.

"You know something."

He pointed at the registry. Then at the ceiling. Then at the west wing.

Bai Su's stomach tightened. "The west wing? The suicide candidate?"

The ghost nodded once, water streaming from his hair in rivers that evaporated before they hit the floor.

She'd met the suicide candidate exactly once. A girl named Lin Yue, who died by hanging from the west wing's central beam. The academy masters said she couldn't bear the pressure of the exams. But when Bai Su had seen her, swaying gently in the drafty corridor, her neck bent at an impossible angle—she'd tried to speak. Her voice came out like wind through dead leaves.

"Not my choice," Lin Yue had whispered. "Not my choice."

Bai Su rolled the scroll and stood. Her legs had fallen asleep. She hobbled toward the door, grabbing a shelf for support. The wood groaned.

"I need to see her again."

---

The west wing at night was a different world.

Moonlight filtered through cracked windows, casting everything in shades of silver and shadow. The floorboards here were warped, rising and falling like the deck of a ship. Bai Su counted her steps. Forty-three from the library entrance to the central beam. Forty-three steps too many.

Lin Yue hung there now.

She wasn't alone.

Three other ghosts stood beneath her, their faces upturned. One was the murdered prodigy—a boy named Chen Wei who'd been found with a calligraphy brush jammed through his eye. Another was a girl Bai Su hadn't seen before, her uniform torn, her chest caved in like something had crushed her from inside. The third was a man. Old. His scholar's robe was centuries out of fashion, his beard long and gray.

He turned when Bai Su entered.

"So you're the one who sees." His voice was gravel and age. "I wondered when you'd come."

"You knew I'd find the registry?"

"I knew you'd find something." The old man floated closer. His feet didn't touch the ground. "You have that look. The one that asks questions instead of memorizing answers. The academy hates that look."

Bai Su's hand went to her belt pouch. She'd started carrying powdered incense—a trick from a ghost in the library. They could smell it. It calmed them.

"I need to know about the deaths," she said. "The ones that happen three days before exams."

Silence.

Chen Wei, the murdered prodigy, drifted toward her. His remaining eye was bright. Furious.

"He doesn't want you to know," Chen Wei said. "The old one. He's been here longest. He's seen the most."

The old ghost's face twisted. "Because knowing won't save you. It'll only get you killed faster."

"I'm already walking around a school full of dead students," Bai Su said. "Pretty sure I passed 'killed faster' the day I enrolled."

Lin Yue's rope creaked. She swung slightly, her broken neck making her head loll at a sickening angle. When she spoke, her voice was barely a thread.

"Master Zhang."

The old ghost flinched.

"His name was Master Zhang," Lin Yue continued. "He taught classics. He died forty years ago. But he doesn't stay dead."

Bai Su's blood went cold. "What do you mean, he doesn't stay dead?"

Master Zhang—the old ghost—sank lower, his feet touching the floor. For the first time, Bai Su noticed he was more solid than the others. His robes had color. His face had detail. He looked almost... alive.

"I was a teacher," he said. "A good one. My students passed at rates no other master could match. The academy praised me. Rewarded me. Then one year, I had a student who was better than me. Smarter. More ambitious."

His hands curled into fists.

"I didn't mean to kill him. I only wanted to slow him down. A little push. A broken leg. He'd miss one exam and take the next. But the fall broke his neck instead."

Bai Su's throat closed.

"I panicked," Master Zhang said. "I hid the body. I told everyone he ran away. And then... I realized how easy it was. How simple. One student dies, and suddenly the class rankings shift. My pass rates stayed perfect. The academy stayed happy."

"You've been killing students for forty years?" Bai Su's voice cracked.

"Not me." His eyes flashed. "Not anymore. I've been dead for forty years. But someone else took my place. Someone who figured out my methods. My schedule. My... system."

The other ghosts stirred. Their forms flickered.

"Three days before exams," Lin Yue whispered. "Always the top student. Always a tragedy."

Bai Su thought of the registry. Thirty-seven names. Thirty-seven deaths. "If you're dead, who's doing it now?"

Master Zhang's face went pale. Paler than it already was.

"I don't know. I've tried to find them. Follow them. But every time I get close, they vanish. Like they know I'm watching." He looked at her, and for the first time, she saw fear in his dead eyes. "Like they're a ghost too."

The library bell rang.

One toll. Two. Three.

Bai Su counted. Seven tolls total. Seven in the evening. The dinner hour was over. Soon the night patrol would start, and students caught out of their rooms would face punishment.

"I have to go."

"No." Lin Yue's hand shot out, grabbing Bai Su's sleeve. Her fingers were cold. Wet. "Stay. He's coming."

"Who?"

The temperature dropped.

Bai Su's breath misted. The oil lamps along the corridor flickered, one by one, until only the moonlight remained. The ghosts huddled together, their forms shrinking, their faces twisted in terror.

Even Master Zhang backed away.

A shadow moved at the end of the hall.

It wasn't a person. It wasn't a ghost. It was something between—a shape that swallowed light, that made the air itself feel sick. It moved slowly. Deliberately. Each step left a darkness that didn't fade.

Bai Su's legs wouldn't move.

"Don't look at it," Chen Wei hissed. "Don't let it know you can see."

But she couldn't look away.

The shape stopped. Turned. And she felt something gaze back at her—something ancient and hungry and patient.

Then the shadow spoke.

"New student."

The voice came from everywhere. From the walls. From the floor. From inside her own skull.

"You see me."

Bai Su's hand went to her belt pouch. The incense. She threw a handful of powder into the air.

The shadow recoiled.

For one second, she saw what it hid—a face. Young. Male. His eyes were white. His mouth was sewn shut with black thread. His skin was pale as paper, cracked like old porcelain.

Then the darkness swallowed him again.

"Smart," the voice said. "But incense won't save you. Nothing will. You see too much. Ask too many questions. And three days from now..."

The shadow began to fade.

"You'll join us."

The lamps returned. The temperature rose. The ghosts were gone—all of them, vanished into the walls like they'd never existed.

Bai Su stood alone in the west wing, shaking.

Three days.

Three days until the imperial examinations.

Three days until someone died.

And she was the top student in this month's assessments.

title: The Bloodstained Examination Scroll
content:
The morning bell never rang.

Bai Su noticed it first—the unnatural silence that settled over Wutong Academy like a shroud. No brass bell clanging from the east tower. No hurried footsteps of students racing to morning assembly. Just the wind scraping through empty corridors and the distant caw of crows circling the library roof.

She found the other living students gathered in the dining hall, their faces pale as rice paper. Lin Wei stood at the center, her fingers wrapped around a cup of tea so tightly the porcelain creaked.

"The bell ringer is dead," Lin Wei said.

Bai Su's stomach dropped. "Which one?"

"The living one."

The words hung in the air like smoke. There were two bell ringers at Wutong—one living student who pulled the rope during daylight hours, and one ghost who took over after sunset. The living bell ringer was a third-year named Chen Feng. Quiet boy. Kept to himself. Never made trouble.

"Found him in the east tower," Lin Wei continued. "His neck was broken. The rope was still wrapped around his hands."

"He fell?"

"He was pushed." Lin Wei set down her tea with a click that echoed through the silent hall. "The ghost bell ringer saw it happen. Said a shadow came up behind Chen Feng and shoved him through the window. Three stories down."

Bai Su's skin crawled. She had met the ghost bell ringer once—a thin, nervous spirit who had died of exhaustion during the Tang Dynasty, condemned to pull the bell rope for eternity. He spoke in whispers and avoided eye contact. If he said he saw something, he meant it.

"Did he recognize the shadow?" asked a boy with ink-stained fingers.

"No face. Just a shape. But it moved like a scholar." Lin Wei's voice dropped. "And it was humming."

The room went cold. Every living student knew that detail meant something, though none of them wanted to say what.

Bai Su's mind raced. Humming. The drowned scholar in the library always hummed folk songs from his home village. The suicide candidate in the west wing hummed when he was nervous. But there was another ghost who hummed—the one she had glimpsed in the archives, the one who disappeared before she could get a good look.

"Where's the body now?" she asked.

"Headmaster's office. They're sending word to his family." Lin Wei's composure cracked for just a moment. "He was supposed to take the mock exam next week. He'd been studying for months."

Bai Su excused herself before anyone could ask questions she didn't want to answer.

The east tower stood at the edge of the academy grounds, a crumbling stone structure that predated the main buildings by centuries. Its spiral staircase wound upward in uneven steps, worn smooth by generations of feet—living and dead. Bai Su climbed slowly, her hand trailing along the cold stone wall, feeling for any residual warmth that might tell her something.

She found nothing.

At the top, the bell chamber was empty. The great brass bell hung silent in its wooden frame, the rope dangling motionless beside the shattered window. Shards of glass glittered on the floor like frozen tears. Bai Su knelt and examined them, careful not to cut herself.

A single footprint pressed into the dust—too small for a man, too precisely placed for a ghost.

She measured it with her own foot. Half the size. Delicate. A woman's shoe.

"Clever girl."

Bai Su spun around. The ghost student stood in the doorway—the one who had been watching her since her first day. He was young, maybe eighteen, with sharp features and eyes that held no warmth. His scholar's robes were immaculate, not a thread out of place.

"You're following me," she said.

"I'm protecting you." He stepped into the chamber, his feet leaving no marks. "There's a difference."

"Protecting me from what?"

"The living are dying, Bai Su. And you can see us." He tilted his head, studying her like she was a difficult passage in a text. "That makes you a target."

"You know who killed Chen Feng."

"I know who killed all of them."

Bai Su's breath caught. "All of them?"

The ghost scholar walked to the window and gazed out at the courtyard below. "Three living students have died this semester. Chen Feng makes four. Each death was ruled an accident—a fall, a drowning, a poisoning from bad food. But they were all murdered."

"By who?"

"A ghost who has forgotten what it means to be dead." He turned to face her, and for the first time, Bai Su saw something like fear in his expression. "There is a spirit in this academy who believes that life can be taken. That if they steal enough living qi, they can become human again."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" He gestured at the bell, the rope, the shattered window. "Chen Feng had enough life force to power a small village. That much qi doesn't just disappear. It was taken. Consumed."

Bai Su's hands trembled. She clenched them into fists to make it stop. "Who?"

"I don't know their name. They hide well. But I know where they study."

"Where?"

"The restricted archives. Fourth floor. The section that's been sealed since the academy was founded."

Bai Su had heard rumors about the restricted archives. Every student had. It was where they kept the texts too dangerous for young scholars—theories about resurrection, forbidden alchemy, the kind of knowledge that had gotten people executed in older dynasties.

"Why haven't you told the headmaster?"

The ghost scholar laughed, a hollow sound that echoed off the stone walls. "The headmaster is dead, Bai Su. He's been dead for three hundred years. He just doesn't know it yet."

The words hit her like a physical blow. "That's not—"

"Check his pulse. Check his shadow." The ghost's voice softened. "Wutong Academy isn't a school for the living and the dead. It's a school for the dead who are too stubborn to leave. The headmaster, the teachers, half the staff—they're all ghosts. They just don't remember dying."

Bai Su backed away until her shoulders hit the wall. The world tilted, reality shifting like sand beneath her feet. She thought of Headmaster Zhao's papery skin, his brittle laugh, the way he never seemed to blink. She thought of her first day, when he had welcomed her to Wutong and promised her a bright future.

"Then why am I here?" she whispered. "Why are any of us living students here?"

"Because the ghosts need you." The scholar's eyes burned with something ancient and desperate. "The living are the only ones who can still change things. We're locked in our patterns, repeating our deaths over and over. But you—you can choose differently."

"That doesn't make sense."

"It will." He stepped closer, close enough that Bai Su could see the faint translucence of his skin, the way light passed through him like water through silk. "But first, you need to find the killer. Before they take enough qi to break through the veil entirely."

"What happens if they do?"

"They won't just become human again." He leaned in, his voice dropping to a whisper. "They'll become something worse. A ghost that remembers how to live is a ghost that remembers how to kill. And they will not stop until every living student in this academy is dead."

The bell above them rang.

Once.

Twice.

Three times.

Bai Su looked up, expecting to see the ghost bell ringer pulling the rope. But the chamber was empty. The rope hung still. And yet the bell continued to ring, a deep, mournful toll that shook the dust from the rafters.

"It's starting," the ghost scholar said.

"What's starting?"

He didn't answer. When she turned back, he was gone.

The bell rang four times. Five. Six. Bai Su counted each one, her heart pounding in time with the vibrations. Seven. Eight. Nine. The sound grew louder, more frantic, as if the bell itself was screaming.

On the tenth ring, it stopped.

Silence crashed down around her, heavy and suffocating. Bai Su stood alone in the bell chamber, surrounded by broken glass and the ghost of a murdered boy. She looked down at her hands.

They were covered in blood.

Not hers. Not fresh. Old blood, dried and cracked, staining her palms like rust. She had no memory of touching anything that could have left this mark. No memory of anything at all since she had entered the tower.

The footprint in the dust caught her eye again. Delicate. Precise.

She looked at the sole of her shoe.

It matched.

Bai Su's blood ran cold. She stumbled backward, her mind screaming denials that her eyes refused to accept. The footprint was hers. She had been here before. She had stood at this window, looked down at the courtyard, watched Chen Feng fall—

"Miss Bai?"

She whirled around. A servant girl stood in the doorway, her face pale with concern.

"Miss Bai, the headmaster is asking for you. He says it's urgent."

Bai Su looked at her hands again.

The blood was gone.

She wiped them on her robes anyway, her skin crawling with phantom warmth.

"Tell him I'll be right there."

The servant bowed and disappeared down the stairs. Bai Su took one last look at the bell chamber—the shattered window, the dangling rope, the footprint that was no longer there—and followed.

She didn't see the shadow that detached itself from the wall behind her.

She didn't hear the whisper that followed her down the stairs.

But she felt it.

A cold breath on the back of her neck.

And a voice that sang an old folk song, sweet and familiar, like something she had known before she was born.
